The deeper story behind Python’s most familiar features and why mastering them will make you a better developer.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Most Python developers become productive within weeks. Its clean syntax, extensive standard library, and readable code make it one of the easiest programming languages to learn. But beneath that simplicity lies a sophisticated runtime with carefully designed features that many developers use every day without fully understanding.
This article explores ten such features. You don’t need to memorize every implementation detail, but understanding why these features work the way they do will help you write cleaner, faster, and more maintainable Python code.
1. Variables Don’t Store Values — They Store References
Many beginners imagine variables as boxes that contain data. In Python, variables are better thought of as labels attached to objects.
Consider this example:
a = [1, 2, 3]
b = aAt this point, both a and b refer to the same list object.
b.append(4)
print(a)Output:
[1, 2, 3, 4]
